exemplar.title: To what extent is gender correlated with the carbon footprint of students in a secondary international school in Hong Kong?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent is gender correlated with the carbon footprint of students in a secondary international school in Hong Kong?

subject.title: Environmental systems and societies (ESS - Old)
subject.description: IB Environmental Systems and Societies (ESS) examines environmental challenges through an interdisciplinary approach combining natural sciences with social and economic perspectives. Students study ecosystems, biodiversity, resource management, pollution, climate change, and sustainability using systems thinking and collaborative investigation. The course develops environmental literacy and critical thinking skills essential for understanding and addressing complex environmental issues facing our planet.

strengths: Consistent use of appropriate technical terminology
Logical overall structure with clear headings and supported figures
weaknesses: Long text blocks, duplicate references and inconsistent citation style disrupt flow
Cluttered data tables in the main text and misaligned captions hamper readability
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Communication is generally clear but hampered by organizational inconsistencies and lack of concision, matching the descriptor for band 2 and warranting a score of 2.

strengths: Clearly framed research question specifying independent and dependent variables within a defined population
Wellâdeveloped environmental context with credible sources linking meat consumption and urbanization to emissions
weaknesses: Population definition remains somewhat broad without specifying exact year levels
Lacks deeper quantitative context (e.g., systems diagram or emission trend data)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two of the three strands are firmly in the top band (5â6) and the third is high in the 3â4 band. Minor phrasing issues and absence of richer quantitative context prevent the maximum, but overall coherence and linkage to Hong Kongâs environmental issue merit a holistic midâhigh score of 5.

strengths: Method is clearly described and repeatable with defined scoring and statistical test
Variables are explicitly identified, supporting replicability
weaknesses: No pilot test or validation of the survey scale
Convenience sampling bias and lack of alternative strategy discussion
Risk assessment lacks formal matrix and omits parental consent details
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The method design is repeatable (band 3â4), but insufficient validation and sampling/ethical omissions limit the score to a solid band-3 performance (3).

strengths: Findings are linked to wider contexts and supported by external literature
Practical limitations and realistic improvements (e.g., anonymity, incentives) are proposed
weaknesses: Evaluation of the conclusion against the original environmental issue is brief
No discussion of measurement validity or reliability
Suggested extensions lack major redesign or systemâlevel interventions
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Describes strengths, weaknesses and offers meaningful improvements, placing this clearly in the 3â4 band but toward the lower end due to depth omissions, hence a score of 3.

strengths: Correct presentation of processed data tables and one wellâlabelled bar chart
Accurate calculation and application of tâtest leading to a valid rejection of Hâ
weaknesses: Multiple data displays (e.g., pie charts, error bars) are missing, omitting relevant information
Contradictory t statistic direction and lack of confidence intervals hinder clarity
Conclusion wording overreaches by implying causation instead of correlation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although statistical calculations are correct, significant omissions in data presentation and interpretative precision constrain the work to the lower half of band 3â4, leading to a holistic score of 3.

strengths: Proposes a plant-based dietary shift grounded in findings
Identifies key strengths (reduced GHG, water use) and weaknesses (cultural barriers)
weaknesses: Justification remains generic without economic or policy feasibility analysis
Omission of infrastructure or implementation considerations
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Solution is described with some justification and partial evaluation of pros and cons, aligning squarely with band 2 criteria, so a score of 2 is awarded.

strandSelections.reasoning: Terminology such as âindependent variableâ, ât-testâ, âcarbon footprintâ is used consistently, yet informal phrasing (âvery simpleâ, âhuge factorâ) reduces academic concision.  This meets âeither uses appropriate terminology or is conciseââband 2.

The student links findings to wider contexts (age groups, global comparisons) and cites a 2024 study, but evaluation of how well the conclusion addresses the original environmental issue is brief.  Some omissions (no discussion of systemic impact) place it in band 3-4, lower value 3.

The student states that the null hypothesis is rejected, quantifies the mean difference, and relates this back to gendered consumption patterns.  Evidence from the t-test is cited, so the conclusion is valid and supported.  Minor over-generalisations ("confirmed by the data that genderâ¦") prevent full marks but band 3-4, high score 4, is justified.

Means, standard deviations and a two-sample t-test are correctly calculated; assumptions are acknowledged (Î± = 0.05, df).  The analysis is statistically appropriate but limited to a single test and descriptive comparison.  It is correct yet incomplete, matching band 3-4, upper score 4.

Suggested improvements (increase questions, ensure anonymity, offer incentives) address key weaknesses and are realistic.  They are meaningful but not far-reaching (no redesign of footprint scale or alternative sampling).  Thus, band 3-4, score 3.

The on-line survey method, scoring scheme (1â4), averaging procedure and planned t-test are explained well enough that another researcher could replicate them.  Nevertheless, only seven questions are used and no pilot test or validation of the scale is provided, so sufficiency is questionable.  This aligns with the middle band (3-4); the method is repeatable but not fully appropriate, hence 4.

Connections between gender, lifestyle choices and footprint are described with several examples (transport habits, diet, body-mass-linked caloric demand).  However, causal pathways are asserted rather than fully explained and no systems perspective is offered.  These omissions fit the 3-4 band, and the detail provided merits the upper value of 4.

The research question is stated clearly, is narrowly bounded to one school population, and contains both the IV (gender) and DV (carbon-footprint score).  It is neither compound nor ambiguous, so focus and coherence are strong.  Minor phrasing issues (e.g. repeated word âgenderâ in the conclusion) do not detract from clarity, therefore the descriptor for 5â6 is best-matched; a mid-band score of 5 is appropriate rather than the maximum, because wording could be tightened and the exact population (all secondary year levels?) is still somewhat broad.

Strengths (lower GHG emissions, lower water use) and weaknesses (cultural barriers, nutrient gaps) are evaluated, yet economic or policy feasibility is not explored.  Evaluation is partial, fitting band 2 with full score 2.

Headings and logical chronology are maintained, but long blocks of text, repeated tables, and appendix placement occasionally disrupt flow.  Organisation is âmainlyâ but not fully sustainedâdescriptor for band 2 fits.

Strengths (quick survey, adequate responses) and multiple limitations (sample size, honesty of responses, limited question breadth) are described.  However, discussion lacks depth on statistical limitations and measurement validity.  This meets âdescribes strengths, weaknesses and limitationsââband 3-4 with score 3.

The student discusses Hong Kongâs exceptionally high per-capita footprint, cites meat consumption, rapid urbanisation and WWF data, and links these to global warming.  Sources are cited.  While the depth could be richer (no systems diagrams, little quantitative data), the issue is nevertheless discussed rather than merely outlined, satisfying the top-band descriptor; a score of 5 recognises some gaps in quantitative depth.

Convenience sampling is named and some justification (access and voluntary participation) is offered, but the resultant bias and its implications are not explored, nor are alternative strategies considered.  This fulfils âdescribesâ rather than âjustifiesâ, matching band 3-4 but at the lower end (3).

Ethical points (confidentiality, voluntary participation, social desirability bias) are listed and briefly elaborated, but there is no structured risk matrix or reference to institutional consent.  The coverage is more than a list yet still lacks comprehensive depth, so a band-3 score of 3 is warranted.

Raw and processed data tables plus one bar chart are provided; figures are labelled.  However, many charts (e.g. pie charts) are absent for other questions and no error bars accompany means, so some relevant data are omitted.  This fits âappropriate diagrams/charts with some omissionsââband 3-4, lower value 3.

One solutionâshifting to plant-based dietsâis described with reference to form data and external sources; justification is present but somewhat generic.  This matches the descriptor âdescribes one potential application/solution with weak justificationâ, i.e. band 2.

Argument generally follows a logical progression from context to conclusion, but occasional digressions and minor redundancies (duplicate citations) hamper reader ease.  This is âmainly logical but difficult to follow in partsâ, matching band 2.

annotations.comment: The research question is clearly framed, identifying both the independent (gender) and dependent (carbon footprint) variables, and specifying a focused school population. This coherence supports strong contextual identification.
Summarize individual averages using descriptive statistics (e.g., mean, median, range) rather than full tables to improve coherence and conciseness.
Table 2.0âs caption appears misaligned below a blank row and separated from its data. Ensure captions directly follow and describe the associated table.
The phrasing of the research question could be tightened by specifying exact year levels and avoiding redundant wording. Narrowing the defined population will enhance focus and reduce ambiguity.

The environmental context paragraph effectively situates Hong Kongâs per-capita footprint within regional and global rankings, citing credible sources and linking meat consumption and urbanization to rising emissions.
To deepen context, include a systems diagram or quantitative analysis (e.g., per-capita emission trends) to illustrate feedback loops between meat consumption, energy use, and urban density.
The reliance on studies of single adults in Sweden may not translate to a Hong Kong school context. The student should explicitly relate external findings to the target population.
Variables are clearly defined (IV, DV, CV), aiding replicability. The structured listing allows another researcher to understand the design framework at a glance.
Labeling âstudents at school attendedâ as a control variable is too vague. The student should specify demographic or behavioural controls to reduce confounding factors.
Including Figures 1.0 and 1.1 to illustrate survey question format strengthens communication of instrumentation and enhances methodological clarity.
The procedure lacks evidence of a pilot test or validation of the 1â4 scale to confirm that survey questions accurately measure carbon-footprint behaviours.
Consider distributing the survey via multiple channels (e.g., school newsletter, social media) to ensure broader participation in case the bulletin posting is delayed.
Convenience sampling is named and justified by access, but the student omits discussion of resulting bias and representativeness implications.
A formal risk matrix outlining likelihood and impact of ethical issues, plus institutional consent requirements, would strengthen the risk assessment.
Ethical considerations omit discussion of obtaining parental consent or assent for minors. Including these would ensure full compliance with research ethics.
Separate the rationale for expecting a gender difference from the hypothesis statement. Use precise LaTeX: e.g., $$H_0: 9_m = 9_f$$ and $$H_1: 9_m > 9_f$$.
The formula $$\overline{x} = \frac{\Sigma X}{N}$$ is correctly displayed, and definitions for \Sigma X and N clarify the calculation procedure.
The reported t statistic (â2.14) contradicts the direction of the alternate hypothesis; a negative value suggests female means exceed male means.
Adding confidence intervals or error bars to the t-test results would visually convey variability and strengthen interpretation of statistical significance.
The interpretation that p = 0.0187 < 0.05 justifies rejecting Hâ is correct and demonstrates valid application of the significance threshold.
Figure 2.0 lacks error bars to show the standard deviations discussed in the text. Including these would enhance readersâ understanding of data spread.
Discussing data spread without citing the numerical SD values reduces analytical rigor. Refer to the 0.35 and 0.37 SDs to support the narrative.
The conclusion uses causal phrasing (âsignificant effectâ) instead of correlational language. Revise to state a significant correlation to maintain accuracy.
The discussion broadens relevance by comparing age groups and citing Zhang (2024), demonstrating evaluation of findings in wider contexts.
To deepen the evaluation, discuss how findings could inform school environmental policies or broader system-level interventions.
The evaluation notes sample size limitations but neglects discussion of the surveyâs validity and reliability. Address measurement quality to complete the evaluation.
Practical and actionable improvementsâensuring anonymity and offering incentivesâare realistic steps to enhance response honesty and sample size.
The application proposal linking plant-based diets to reduced GHG and water use effectively uses findings to justify a solution.
The solution evaluation omits economic feasibility and policy barriers. Discuss cost, cultural acceptance, and infrastructure to strengthen appraisal.
The works cited list contains duplicate Euronews entries. Remove redundant references to improve accuracy and avoid confusion.
Citations mix URLs and author-title formats inconsistently. Adopt a single citation style (APA or MLA) throughout for professional communication.
Presenting every individual average calculation within the main report clutters the narrative. Relocate detailed tables to the appendix.
